subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: In this unit your child learns about materials - the stuff that things are made from. Look around your home. A spoon is made from metal. A cup may be made from plastic. A door is made from wood. A window is made from glass. A dress is made from cloth. A book is made from paper. The wall of a house in Lamu is made from stone. Every single thing is made from a material, and each material feels and behaves in its own way.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the material an everyday object is made from.
Describe materials using words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy and stiff.
Sort objects by their material or by a property.
Say why a material is chosen for a particular job.
Test and compare how different materials feel and behave.
Observe and record the properties of materials.

concepts.title: What Things Are Made Of
concepts.explanation: Start by helping your child understand the word material. A material is what an object is made from. Hold up a wooden spoon and a metal spoon side by side. They do the same job, but they are made from different materials. Sit together with the basket of objects. Pick up one object at a time.
concepts.example: Name the material clearly. Say it, then let your child say it back.

concepts.title: Hard and Soft
concepts.explanation: A property is a describing word for a material - how it feels or what it can do. Young children learn properties best through their fingers. Let them touch, press, and gently bend. Teach these pairs of opposite words:
concepts.example: A property is a describing word for a material - how it feels or what it can do. Young children learn properties best through their fingers. Let them touch, press, and gently bend. Teach these pairs of opposite words:

concepts.title: Rough and Smooth
concepts.explanation: Now help your child see why we choose a material. People do not build a window from wood - you could not see through it. People do not make a cooking pot from paper - it would burn and fall apart. We match the material to the job because of what the material can do. Talk through simple, familiar examples from home:
concepts.example: A metal sufuria is used for cooking because metal is hard and does not burn on the fire.

concepts.title: Bendy and Stiff
concepts.explanation: Sorting means putting things into groups that are the same in some way. Sorting is a big science skill, and children of this age love it. You can sort the same basket of objects in many ways. Why does sorting matter so much? When your child puts all the metal things together, they are noticing what those things share. That is the beginning of real scientific thinking - looking for what is the same and what is different. Grown-up scientists do exactly this when they study the world. Your child is learning to think…
concepts.example: By material: all the wood things here, all the metal things there.

concepts.title: Sorting Materials
concepts.explanation: Sorting Materials. Explore this idea together by looking, doing and talking about what you notice.

concepts.title: Choosing the Right Material
concepts.explanation: Choosing the Right Material. Explore this idea together by looking, doing and talking about what you notice.

explorations.prompt: What do you predict will happen in Which Material Is Waterproof??
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.

steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

activities.title: Will It Float or Sink?
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of bendy and stiff around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Build the Strongest Tower
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of sorting materials around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort Toys by Material
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of choosing the right material around your home or outside.
Change one thing, keep everything else the same, and record what happens.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Does not press in. It stays firm.
Presses in easily. It feels gentle.
Flat and even, with no bumps.
Will not bend. It keeps its shape.

vocabulary.meaning: Does not press in. It stays firm.
vocabulary.example: To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.

vocabulary.meaning: Presses in easily. It feels gentle.
vocabulary.example: To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.
To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.

vocabulary.meaning: Flat and even, with no bumps.
vocabulary.example: To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.
To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.

vocabulary.meaning: Will not bend. It keeps its shape.
vocabulary.example: To describe materials with words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy, and stiff.

commonMistakes: Some hard things snap easily — glass is hard but it breaks.
Some metal things, like kitchen foil, are very light.

connections.text: Bendy and stiff materials behave differently when you push and pull them.
Look around the kitchen: every object's material was chosen to do its job well.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Bendy and Stiff.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Sorting means putting things into groups that are the same in some way. Sorting is a big science skill, and children of this age love it. You can sort the same basket of objects in many ways. Why does sorting matter so much? When your child puts all the metal things together, they are noticing what those things share. That is the beginning of real scientific thinking - looking for what is the same and what is different. Grown-up scientists do exactly this when they study the world. Your child is learning to think…

questions.question: A pillow feels...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A pillow is soft, so it is nice to rest on.

questions.question: A stone feels...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A stone is hard. It does not squash.

questions.question: Which material is bendy?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A rubber band bends easily, so it is bendy.

questions.question: Sandpaper feels...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Sandpaper is rough and scratchy to touch.

questions.question: Glass is usually...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.

questions.question: Which is best for a warm blanket?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Soft cloth is warm and comfy, so it is best for a blanket.

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Does not press in. It stays firm.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Hard means Does not press in. It stays firm..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Presses in easily. It feels gentle.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Soft means Presses in easily. It feels gentle..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Bumpy and uneven to touch.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Rough means Bumpy and uneven to touch..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Flat and even, with no bumps.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Smooth means Flat and even, with no bumps..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Can bend without breaking.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Bendy means Can bend without breaking..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: Will not bend. It keeps its shape.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Stiff means Will not bend. It keeps its shape..

rounds.explanation: A pillow is soft, so it is nice to rest on.

rounds.prompt: Sandpaper feels...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Sandpaper is rough and scratchy to touch.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: Does not press in. It stays firm.?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Hard means Does not press in. It stays firm..

games.title: Mastery Mix: Bendy
games.description: Practise bendy through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: Can bend without breaking.?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Bendy means Can bend without breaking..

rounds.prompt: A stone feels...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: A stone is hard. It does not squash.

rounds.prompt: Glass is usually...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Glass feels smooth and flat.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: Presses in easily. It feels gentle.?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Soft means Presses in easily. It feels gentle..

games.title: Unit Champion: Stiff
games.description: Practise stiff through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: Will not bend. It keeps its shape.?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Stiff means Will not bend. It keeps its shape..

rounds.prompt: Which material is bendy?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: A rubber band bends easily, so it is bendy.

rounds.prompt: Which is best for a warm blanket?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Soft cloth is warm and comfy, so it is best for a blanket.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: Bumpy and uneven to touch.?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Rough means Bumpy and uneven to touch..

selfAssessment: I can name the material an everyday object is made from.
I can describe materials using words like hard, soft, rough, smooth, bendy and stiff.
I can sort objects by their material or by a property.
I can say why a material is chosen for a particular job.
I can test and compare how different materials feel and behave.
I can observe and record the properties of materials.
